The **Chicago American Giants** were a [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago")-based [Negro league baseball](/wiki/Negro_league_baseball "Negro league baseball") team. From 1910 until the mid-1930s, the American Giants were the most dominant team in black baseball. Owned and managed from 1911 to 1926 by [player-manager](/wiki/Player-manager "Player-manager") [Andrew "Rube" Foster](/wiki/Rube_Foster "Rube Foster"), they were charter members of Foster's [Negro National League](/wiki/Negro_National_League_(1920-1931) "Negro National League (1920-1931)"). The American Giants won five pennants in that league, along with another pennant in the 1932 [Negro Southern League](/wiki/Negro_Southern_League_(1920%E2%80%9336) "Negro Southern League (1920–36)") and a second-half championship in Gus Greenlee's Negro National League in 1934.

## Founding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chicago_American_Giants&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Founding")]

In 1910, Foster, captain of the [Chicago Leland Giants](/wiki/Leland_Giants "Leland Giants"), wrestled legal control of the name "Leland Giants" away from the team's owner, [Frank Leland](/wiki/Frank_Leland "Frank Leland"). That season, featuring Hall of Fame shortstop [John Henry Lloyd](/wiki/John_Henry_Lloyd "John Henry Lloyd"), outfielder [Pete Hill](/wiki/Pete_Hill "Pete Hill"), second baseman [Grant Johnson](/wiki/Home_Run_Johnson "Home Run Johnson"), catcher [Bruce Petway](/wiki/Bruce_Petway "Bruce Petway"), and pitcher [Frank Wickware](/wiki/Frank_Wickware "Frank Wickware"), the Leland Giants reportedly won 123 games while losing only 6. In 1911, Foster renamed the club the "American Giants".[[2]](#cite_note-2)

### Franchise continuum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chicago_American_Giants&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Franchise continuum")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/timeline/m49vx6z3pk29tc9nj7wuhsaxmri78vw.png)

|  |
| --- |
| The [Chicago Unions](/wiki/Chicago_Unions "Chicago Unions") and the [Chicago Columbia Giants](/wiki/Chicago_Columbia_Giants "Chicago Columbia Giants") merged for the 1901 season creating the [Chicago Union Giants](/wiki/Leland_Giants "Leland Giants"), who later changed their name to the Leland Giants. The Leland Giants then split into two teams for the 1910 season creating the [Chicago Giants](/wiki/Chicago_Giants "Chicago Giants") and the new Leland Giants, who later changed their name to the **Chicago American Giants**.[[3]](#cite_note-3) |

## Early dominance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chicago_American_Giants&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early dominance")]

For a list of annual win-loss records, see [List of Chicago American Giants seasons](/wiki/List_of_Chicago_American_Giants_seasons "List of Chicago American Giants seasons").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Chicago_American_Giants_1919.jpg/250px-Chicago_American_Giants_1919.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chicago_American_Giants_1919.jpg)

1919 Chicago American Giants

Playing in spacious [Schorling's Park](/wiki/South_Side_Park "South Side Park") (formerly "South Side Park III", the home field of the American League's [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/Chicago_White_Sox "Chicago White Sox")), Foster's club relied on fielding, pitching, speed, and "[inside baseball](/wiki/Inside_Baseball_(strategy) "Inside Baseball (strategy)")" to succeed in the young [Negro National League](/wiki/Negro_National_League_(the_first) "Negro National League (the first)") (NNL), winning championships in 1920, 1921, and 1922. When the [Kansas City Monarchs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Monarchs "Kansas City Monarchs") supplanted the American Giants as the dominant team beginning in 1923, Foster tried rebuilding but by 1926 his health (physical and mental) was failing. Accordingly, his protégé [Dave Malarcher](/wiki/Dave_Malarcher "Dave Malarcher") took over on-field management of the team. Malarcher followed Foster's pattern, emphasizing pitching and defense, and led the American Giants back to the top-tier of the Negro leagues, winning pennants in 1926 and 1927.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Both seasons also saw the American Giants defeat the [Bacharach Giants](/wiki/Bacharach_Giants "Bacharach Giants") of Atlantic City, champions of the Eastern Colored League, in the [Negro League World Series](/wiki/Negro_League_World_Series "Negro League World Series").

## Cole's American Giants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chicago_American_Giants&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Cole's American Giants")]

The NNL collapsed in 1931, and in 1932 the team won the [Negro Southern League](/wiki/Negro_Southern_League_(1920%E2%80%9336) "Negro Southern League (1920–36)") pennant as **Cole's American Giants**. The next season the American Giants joined the new [Negro National League](/wiki/Negro_National_League_(the_second) "Negro National League (the second)"), losing the pennant to the [Pittsburgh Crawfords](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Crawfords "Pittsburgh Crawfords") in a controversial decision by league president Gus Greenlee (owner of the Crawfords). The 1933 season saw the Giants get kicked off of their home field after the end of May; the park owners preferred to use the land as a dog racing track for the remaining summer months. This forced the Giants to play the majority of their home games in [Indianapolis](/wiki/Indianapolis "Indianapolis") for the balance of that season.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In 1934, the American Giants won the NNL's second-half title, then fell to the [Philadelphia Stars](/wiki/Philadelphia_Stars_(baseball) "Philadelphia Stars (baseball)") in a seven-game playoff for the championship. In 1937, after a year spent playing as an independent club, the American Giants became a charter member of yet another circuit, the [Negro American League](/wiki/Negro_American_League "Negro American League").

## Decline and demise

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chicago_American_Giants&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Decline and demise")]

[Ted "Double Duty" Radcliffe](/wiki/Ted_Radcliffe "Ted Radcliffe") was appointed manager in 1950. The team's owner, [Dr. J.B. Martin](/wiki/J._B._Martin "J. B. Martin"), was concerned about black players joining [major league](/wiki/Major_league_baseball "Major league baseball") teams so he instructed Radcliffe to sign white players. Radcliffe recruited at least five young white players ([Lou Chirban](/wiki/Lou_Chirban "Lou Chirban"), [Lou Clarizio](/wiki/Lou_Clarizio "Lou Clarizio"), [Al Dubetts](/w/index.php?title=Al_Dubetts&action=edit&redlink=1 "Al Dubetts (page does not exist)"), [Frank Dyall](/wiki/Frank_Dyall "Frank Dyall"), and [Stanley Miarka](/wiki/Stanley_Miarka "Stanley Miarka")).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Sports entrepreneur [Abe Saperstein](/wiki/Abe_Saperstein "Abe Saperstein") owned the American Giants in 1952, its last season in the Negro American League. Its players were dispersed to the four remaining NAL teams for the 1953 season. After dropping out of the Negro American League in 1952[[7]](#cite_note-7), the American Giants became unaffiliated and turned to barnstorming, playing games in the Midwest. With stadium attendance numbers at Comisky park dwindling to the hundreds and top talent being signed away to the MLB, the team disbanded by the 1955 season[[8]](#cite_note-8), then was revived in 1958, playing throughout the South until 1961[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*].

## Home fields

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chicago_American_Giants&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Home fields")]

The American Giants played at [Schorling's Park](/wiki/South_Side_Park "South Side Park") (1911–1940) and [Perry Stadium](/wiki/Bush_Stadium "Bush Stadium") (Indianapolis) (1933), when Schorling's Park was briefly re-purposed mid-season in 1933. Finally, after a destructive fire at Schorling's Park, they shared [Comiskey Park (I)](/wiki/Comiskey_Park "Comiskey Park") (1941–1950), playing when the White Sox were on the road.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Prior to 1911, the predecessor Union Giants club had played in various small local venues, primarily the first "Schorling's Park", a.k.a. "Auburn Park", at 79th Street and Wentworth Avenue in the Auburn Park neighborhood of Chicago's south side.

## MLB throwback jerseys

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chicago_American_Giants&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: MLB throwback jerseys")]

The [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/Chicago_White_Sox "Chicago White Sox") have honored the American Giants by wearing replica uniforms during regular-season baseball games on several occasions, including July 1, 2007 (at Kansas City), July 26, 2008 (at home vs. Detroit), and July 16, 2011, during the 9th Annual Negro League weekend at Detroit, where the home team also wore the jerseys of the [Detroit Stars](/wiki/Detroit_Stars "Detroit Stars") during the 17th annual Negro League Tribute Game.[[10]](#cite_note-10)
